This section needs to be updated...
I am a PhD student in Political Science at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden. My research examines the political feasibility of environmental policy, with a particular focus on how compensation can increase support for ambitious climate action.

My doctoral research investigates compensation from both elite and public perspectives. One project examines whether compensating industries that oppose climate policy can improve the political feasibility of climate reforms. A second explores how perceptions of deservingness shape public support for compensating groups that may bear the costs of environmental policy, such as unemployed workers and affected local communities.

Alongside my dissertation, I work with Professor Aksel Sundström on the ERC-funded project *FEM-Lead*, conducting a meta-analysis of how natural disasters influence politically relevant outcomes, with a focus on electoral incumbency and institutional trust.

Before beginning my PhD in 2025, I worked for nearly three years as a research assistant in the Departments of Political Science and Economics at the University of Gothenburg. I received an MSc in Political Science, specializing in Environmental Governance and Human Behavior, from the University of Gothenburg in 2023 (thesis awarded Pass with Distinction). I earned a BA in Political Science, with minors in Economics and History, from the University of Denver in 2019, graduating *summa cum laude* and as a member of Phi Beta Kappa.

More broadly, my research interests include environmental politics, public opinion, environmental governance, and collective action. I am an organizer with the Centre for Environmental Political Studies (CEPS) at the University of Gothenburg and have contributed to organizing several research workshops, including *Closing the Legitimacy Gap* at NESS 2026.

Please feel free to get in touch if you would like to discuss my research or potential collaborations.
